front: How would you define success for [Product Feature]?
back:
Start by clarifying the user, the job-to-be-done, and the business objective behind the feature
Then define success across four layers: adoption, engagement, outcome, and guardrails
Clarify goal: who is the user, what job are they trying to do, why are we building now, and is the goal adoption, revenue, efficiency, retention, safety, or quality?
Primary outcome: what is the one thing this feature must improve — time saved, report quality, CME credits earned, reduced clicks, higher renewal rate, etc.
Input metrics: adoption, activation, engagement, retention, workflow impact
Guardrail metrics: error rate, support tickets, latency, clinical safety concerns, trust, dismissals, workflow disruption
Qualitative signal: user interviews, CSAT/NPS, and “would you be disappointed if this went away?”
RSNA example: for an evidence sidebar inside Rad AI Reporting, success is not just whether radiologists see it — it is whether it helps them make faster, more confident reads without adding clicks
Short answer: I’d define success in layers — user outcome, leading indicators, business result, and guardrails. For healthcare AI, I’d always include safety, trust, workflow disruption, and evidence quality

front: Our metrics are down 10%. How would you determine the root cause?
back:
Do not jump to conclusions. First clarify which metric is down, over what time window, and whether the drop is statistically meaningful or expected seasonality
Clarify metric: down 10% compared to what? Is it volume, rate, revenue, usage, retention, conversion, or quality?
Check instrumentation: analytics change, tracking bug, dashboard definition change, data pipeline issue, release changed events
Segment: user type, customer/account, geography, platform/device, product version, acquisition channel, workflow step, cohort
Timeline: did the drop align with a product release, outage, latency issue, pricing change, campaign ending, competitor launch, seasonality, or policy/regulatory change?
Funnel analysis: where did the drop occur — activation, engagement, conversion, retention, renewal?
Qualitative investigation: support tickets, sales/customer success feedback, user interviews, churn notes, app reviews, session recordings if available
RSNA example: if RSNA content engagement dropped 10%, segment by site, subspecialty, reporting platform, content type, product version, and case mix
Short answer: confirm metric and instrumentation, segment the drop, map against timeline events, then validate hypotheses with data and customer feedback before deciding whether this is analytics, product, market, or customer-segment issue

front: What is a major threat or opportunity facing RSNA Ventures in the next 2 years?
back:
Best answer: the opportunity and threat are the same thing — AI vendors are racing to own the radiologist workflow
Opportunity: radiology AI is moving from standalone algorithms into workflow-native platforms
Threat: if RSNA does not become the trusted content and standards layer inside those workflows, commercial vendors will define the experience without RSNA
Opportunity details: radiology has the highest number of FDA-cleared AI products; radiologists face volume growth, knowledge growth, and burnout; vendors like Rad AI, Nuance/Microsoft, Sirona, Aidoc, and Bayer Calantic are racing to own workflow
RSNA unfair assets: peer-reviewed journals, RadLex, RadElement, ATLAS, CME, global trust, and brand
Threat details: workflow vendors could become the default source of clinical guidance; RSNA content could stay trapped in journals/courses; big tech and AI vendors could commoditize content and own the clinician interface; agentic AI reporting tools could surface generic or vendor-controlled evidence instead of RSNA-validated evidence
Strategic line: the next two years are about whether RSNA becomes a workflow-native institution or remains primarily a destination institution
Short answer: the biggest opportunity is making RSNA’s trusted content and standards workflow-native. The biggest threat is that commercial AI platforms become the default knowledge layer for radiology without RSNA

front: How would you go about building a roadmap for [New Product]?
back:
Start with outcomes, not features. A roadmap should express customer and business outcomes, then sequence bets based on evidence, risk, dependencies, and learning
Clarify strategy: why build this, what business goal does it serve, what customer problem does it solve, what RSNA asset does it leverage?
Define users and segments: primary user, buyer, first segment, academic vs private practice, subspecialty, domestic vs international
Discovery: customer interviews, contextual inquiry, workflow mapping, competitive analysis, partner constraints, regulatory/privacy/compliance review
Define success metrics: user outcome, business outcome, workflow metrics, guardrails
Map opportunities: Opportunity Solution Tree, pain points, use cases, jobs-to-be-done
Prioritize: RICE / value-vs-effort / MoSCoW, strategic fit, risk, dependencies, build/buy/partner, regulatory complexity
Sequence: Now = MVP / riskiest-assumption test; Next = pilot expansion; Later = platform scale. Include kill criteria and learning goals
Align stakeholders: engineering, design, partnerships, sales, legal/privacy/regulatory, clinical advisors, leadership
RSNA example: for point-of-care evidence, start narrow — one partner, one workflow, one subspecialty, one measurable outcome; e.g. neuro CT through Rad AI Reporting surfacing 1-3 RSNA citations during dictation
Short answer: build the roadmap from outcomes backwards — clarify strategy/users, run discovery, define success, map opportunities, prioritize by value/effort/risk/fit, then sequence into Now / Next / Later

front: Meta answer for any ambiguous PM case question.
back:
Do not jump into solutions
Clarify the goal
Clarify the user and buyer
Clarify the current baseline and risk profile
Define success
Segment the problem
Prioritize the highest-leverage opportunity or riskiest assumption
Test cheaply before building broadly
Measure with outcome metrics and guardrails
Iterate based on data and customer feedback
Reusable line: before jumping into solutions, I’d want to clarify the goal, the user, the buyer, the current baseline, and the risk profile. Then I’d pick the right framework for the stage we’re in

front: Best answer: how would you determine pricing for a product?
back:
I haven’t been the final owner of pricing decisions, but I have worked on revenue-generating products and understand the process I’d use
I’d treat pricing as a product discovery and business-model exercise, not just a finance exercise
The goal is to understand value created, buyer willingness to pay, competitive alternatives, cost to serve, and the packaging model that makes adoption easiest
Seven-step process: identify buyer and budget, quantify value created, understand alternatives, choose pricing model, test willingness to pay, model cost-to-serve and margins, then pilot and iterate
I wouldn’t price from cost alone. I’d price from value, but sanity-check it against competitive alternatives and operational cost

front: Step 1: separate user, buyer, economic buyer, and beneficiary.
back:
Pricing depends on the buyer, not just the user
User: who uses the product day to day
Buyer: who signs the contract
Economic buyer: whose budget pays for it
Beneficiary: who gets the value
RSNA example — user: radiologist; buyer: health system, radiology practice, reporting vendor, or AI platform; economic buyer: CMIO, radiology chair, innovation budget, CME budget, or vendor budget; beneficiary: radiologist, institution, patient, and RSNA
Senior line: if you don’t know whose budget pays, you don’t have a pricing strategy yet

front: Step 2: quantify the value created.
back:
Ask: what measurable value does the product create?
Time saved: minutes saved per radiologist per day
Revenue created or protected: more studies read, higher renewal rate, better throughput
Cost avoided: fewer support calls, fewer manual reviews, less admin burden
Risk reduced: fewer missed follow-ups, stronger compliance, better auditability
Quality improved: more consistent reporting, better evidence access, better training
RSNA example: if point-of-care content saves 5 minutes per shift or helps earn CME inside workflow, quantify time saved, CME budget substitution, retention/burnout value, and confidence/quality improvement

front: Step 3: understand alternatives and competitors.
back:
Pricing is anchored by what the buyer compares you to
Ask: are they comparing us to another vendor, a manual workflow, CME courses, internal admin time, a reporting platform add-on, or doing nothing?
For RSNA, Rad AI / reporting tools may be workflow comparables
UpToDate may be a point-of-care learning comparable
ACR/RSNA CME courses may be budget comparables
Manual literature search may be the do-nothing alternative
Senior line: the competitor is not always a company — sometimes it is an existing budget line, habit, or manual workaround

front: Step 4: choose the pricing model.
back:
Common pricing models:
Per seat: dollars per radiologist per year
Per institution: annual enterprise license
Usage-based: per study, report, API call, or CME credit earned
Tiered packaging: basic / pro / enterprise
Partner revenue share: vendor sells it, RSNA gets a percentage
Included with membership: drives retention rather than direct revenue
Hybrid: member benefit plus enterprise upsell
For RSNA Ventures, I’d consider member access included or discounted, institutional license for practices/health systems, vendor channel or revenue share through Rad AI/PowerScribe/Sirona, and enterprise tier with analytics, reporting, admin tools, and support

front: Step 5: test willingness to pay.
back:
Don’t ask: would you pay for this? People lie
Better questions: what budget would this come from? What are you paying for the current alternative? Who would need to approve this? At what price would this feel like a no-brainer? At what price would it require CFO approval? What would make this worth renewing?
Useful methods: Van Westendorp price sensitivity, conjoint analysis for package tradeoffs, sales discovery with real buyers, fake-door pricing page, pilot LOIs with pricing language, A/B testing if self-serve
Senior line: willingness-to-pay research should identify the budget owner, approval path, and renewal logic — not just a number

front: Step 6: model costs, margins, and AI-specific cost drivers.
back:
Pricing has to cover cost-to-serve and margin expectations
General costs: engineering, hosting, implementation, support, customer success, sales, legal, security, compliance, and partner revenue share
Content-product costs: licensing, editorial review, content tagging, updates, citation governance
AI-product costs: token costs, vector database/retrieval costs, human review costs, monitoring and eval infrastructure, model safety work
Healthcare costs: privacy, security reviews, BAAs if PHI is touched, audit logs, validation, regulatory/legal review
Senior line: price from value, but never ignore gross margin or implementation burden

front: Step 7: package, pilot, and iterate.
back:
Packaging matters as much as price — avoid selling a single feature; package around the buyer’s job
Possible RSNA packaging: individual access included with premium RSNA membership; practice per-radiologist annual license; enterprise with SSO, admin dashboard, CME reporting, audit logs, analytics; partner API/content integration into Rad AI, PowerScribe, Sirona, etc.
Start with a pilot price, not a forever price
Measure conversion, sales-cycle length, discounting pressure, renewal intent, usage/adoption, value realized, support burden, and gross margin
Revise packaging and pricing before broad launch
Senior line: pilot pricing is a learning instrument, not just an early revenue number

front: RSNA-specific pricing answer for point-of-care products.
back:
For a point-of-care RSNA product, I’d first clarify whether the buyer is the radiologist, institution, or workflow vendor
If sold through Rad AI or another platform, pricing may look like partner revenue share or enterprise licensing
If CME-oriented, the budget might come from professional development, institutional CME spend, or membership value
If decision-support content, the value story is time saved, confidence, consistency, reduced cognitive load, and less manual literature search
If standards/API/content integration, the value may be vendor differentiation, faster implementation, trusted citations, and reduced content-maintenance burden
I’d test packaging with pilot sites before locking price
Senior line: RSNA should price around the value of trusted content in workflow, not around the raw cost of serving articles

front: Honest framing if you haven’t owned pricing directly.
back:
I haven’t been the final pricing owner, but I have owned revenue-generating products and the product inputs that pricing depends on
Those inputs include customer value, segmentation, packaging, adoption, cost-to-serve, launch strategy, and post-launch performance
I’d partner closely with finance, sales, commercial leadership, and legal on the final number
But I’d expect Product to own the value story, buyer segmentation, package logic, and pilot-learning loop
Strong close: pricing is cross-functional, but product should make sure the price reflects the customer value and the way the product will actually be bought, adopted, and renewed

front: RSNA Ventures / Rad AI competitor map — who is partner, competitor, or adjacent?
back:
RSNA Ventures is not Rad AI's owner — Rad AI is an external strategic partner and reporting workflow surface
Rad AI Reporting: genAI radiology reporting platform; RSNA Ventures partnership surfaces RSNA peer-reviewed content inside the reporting workflow. Relationship: current partner surface
Nuance PowerScribe / Microsoft Dragon Copilot: dominant incumbent reporting platform. Relationship: competitive reporting surface to Rad AI, but also a possible future RSNA content distribution partner
Sirona Medical: cloud-native radiology operating system/reporting platform; publicly positions reporting as multimodal, agentic AI. Relationship: competitor/alternative workflow surface
Bayer Calantic: vendor-neutral AI marketplace/orchestration layer for imaging AI apps; has collaborated with Rad AI. Relationship: adjacent platform, possible channel/partner, not direct reporting competitor
Aidoc CARE / aiOS: image-analysis, triage, care-orchestration, foundation-model platform. Relationship: adjacent enterprise AI platform; competes for budget/workflow attention, not same job as reporting
Smart Reporting: structured reporting/templates. Relationship: adjacent/competitive in structured reporting; possible standards/content partner angle
Best mental model: reporting surfaces compete with Rad AI; orchestration and triage platforms are adjacent; RSNA Ventures' moat is trusted content, standards, accreditation, and brand

front: Which radiology workflow competitors are using agentic AI?
back:
Microsoft/Nuance: yes, publicly moving that way — Dragon Copilot for radiologists is positioned as generative, multimodal, and agentic AI integrated with PowerScribe One
Sirona: yes, explicitly positions reporting as built with multimodal, agentic AI; voice commands can trigger workflow actions like mining patient history and synchronizing report/image context
Rad AI: clearly genAI reporting and workflow automation; public positioning emphasizes reporting, impressions, follow-up, and workflow automation more than the phrase “agentic AI”
Aidoc CARE: foundation-model AI and enterprise orchestration; strong AI platform but more triage/image-analysis/care coordination than agentic reporting
Bayer Calantic: AI marketplace/orchestration, not primarily agentic AI; important because it can coordinate third-party AI apps and has partnered with Rad AI
Smart Reporting: structured reporting and templates; not the strongest public agentic-AI story
Interview line: the agentic-AI arms race is most visible in reporting assistants, but RSNA should not chase the label. The product question is: does the AI reduce radiologist cognitive load safely, with evidence, citations, and no extra clicks?

front: Clean interview answer: are these technologies used by Rad AI or competitors?
back:
I’d separate them into workflow surfaces and adjacent AI platforms
Rad AI is not RSNA’s owned product; it is RSNA Ventures’ first strategic partner and reporting surface
Nuance PowerScribe is the major incumbent and a competitive reporting surface, though it could also become a future RSNA content surface
Sirona is another competitive reporting/workflow surface and is leaning hard into multimodal/agentic AI positioning
Bayer Calantic is more of an AI marketplace and orchestration layer — adjacent, and potentially a partner or channel; it has collaborated with Rad AI
Aidoc CARE is more image-analysis, triage, and care orchestration — adjacent enterprise AI, not a direct reporting competitor
So they are not technologies “inside Rad AI.” They are mostly adjacent or competing workflow platforms that RSNA Ventures should understand as partners, competitors, or distribution channels
Senior close: RSNA Ventures wins by being workflow-surface-agnostic — Rad AI first, but the long-term moat is RSNA content and standards showing up in whichever platform radiologists already use

front: Best answer: what PM methodologies do you use?
back:
I choose the methodology based on the product stage and risk profile
For early discovery, I use customer interviews, JTBD, contextual inquiry, and riskiest-assumption testing
For prioritization, I like RICE or value-vs-effort, but I’m careful not to pretend the scores are more precise than they are
For delivery, I’ve used agile ceremonies, but in regulated environments like Dexcom the real model was hybrid agile inside design controls — iterative software development with traceability, risk management, and launch-readiness gates
For AI products, I add evaluation methods around retrieval quality, groundedness, human-in-the-loop review, and guardrail metrics
The senior answer: I’m not religious about frameworks. I pick the right tool for the product stage, risk, and team.

front: Discovery methods: when are they useful and where do they break?
back:
Jobs to Be Done (JTBD): good for understanding real motivation — when X happens, user wants Y, so they can Z. Great for radiology because the job is not “use AI”; it is “finish a high-quality read without adding clicks.” Weakness: can become abstract if not tied to workflow and data
Customer interviews: good for finding pain, language, buying triggers, and objections. Weakness: users ask for solutions, not problems — listen for behavior, not opinions
Contextual inquiry: strongest in healthcare because workflow matters; sitting in a reading room beats 20 abstract interviews. Weakness: slower to schedule and can create observation bias
Opportunity Solution Tree: good for mapping outcome → opportunity → solution → experiment. Weakness: can feel academic if not grounded in evidence
Riskiest Assumption Testing: good for 0→1 validation; test what must be true before building. Weakness: helps decide whether to build, not whether it is clinically safe or compliant
Design Thinking: good for ambiguity and cross-functional alignment. Weakness: can become workshop theater if not tied to feasibility, business model, and outcomes

front: Prioritization methods: when are they useful and where do they break?
back:
RICE: good for transparent prioritization using Reach, Impact, Confidence, Effort. Weakness: false precision — scores can look objective while being made up
MoSCoW: good for MVP scope and launch readiness — Must, Should, Could, Won’t. Weakness: stakeholders try to label everything Must; PM discipline matters
Value vs. Effort: good for fast visual tradeoff discussions. Weakness: too simplistic for regulatory, security, clinical, or partner risk
Kano: good for distinguishing basic expectations, performance features, and delighters. Weakness: less useful for infrastructure, compliance, or B2B buyer decisions
WSJF: good for enterprise environments where cost of delay matters. Weakness: heavyweight and often overkill for small startup teams
Interview line: I use prioritization methods to structure the conversation, not to outsource judgment

front: Delivery methods: Agile, Kanban, Dual-Track, Waterfall, and regulated hybrid.
back:
Agile / Scrum: good for iterative software development, sprint planning, backlog management, and regular feedback. Weakness: can become ceremony-heavy if teams focus on rituals over outcomes
Kanban: good for continuous flow, support queues, operational work, and release management. Weakness: less useful when fixed sprint commitments or coordinated milestones are required
Dual-Track Agile: good when discovery and delivery run in parallel — PM validates what to build while engineering ships validated work. Weakness: collapses if discovery is fake or rushed
Waterfall / Stage-Gate: good for regulated, hardware-heavy, or approval-heavy work where verification, validation, and traceability matter. Weakness: too slow when requirements are unknown
Hybrid Agile + Design Controls: best answer for Dexcom and healthcare. Iterative software development inside a formal quality system — traceability, risk management, design inputs/outputs, V&V, and launch-readiness gates
Interview line: Dexcom taught me that you can move iteratively without losing regulatory discipline

front: Strategy and roadmap methods: what are they good for?
back:
OKRs: good for aligning teams around outcomes, not tasks. Weakness: bad when used as a task list or vanity metric system
North Star Metric: good for creating one organizing success metric. Weakness: dangerous in healthcare if one metric hides safety or quality tradeoffs — needs guardrails
Strategy stack: mission → strategy → roadmap → execution. Good for senior PM interviews because it connects product work to business and mission. Weakness: vague if not tied to customer evidence
Now / Next / Later roadmap: good for uncertain environments because it communicates direction without overcommitting dates. Weakness: enterprise customers may still need date-based commitments
Outcome-based roadmap: good for framing roadmap around impact, not features. Weakness: sales, support, and implementation teams still need concrete deliverables
Interview line: I like outcome-based roadmaps for leadership alignment and date-based launch plans for execution discipline

front: Metrics and evaluation frameworks: what should you use?
back:
AARRR funnel: good for growth products — Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue. Weakness: less natural for clinical workflow products unless adapted
HEART: good for UX/product experience — Happiness, Engagement, Adoption, Retention, Task Success. Weakness: does not capture clinical safety, regulatory, or business-model risk
North Star + input metrics: good for separating the main outcome from controllable drivers. Weakness: needs guardrails so the team does not over-optimize one metric
Guardrail metrics: essential in healthcare AI — improve time-to-report, but do not increase error rate or workflow disruption. Weakness: can slow decisions if every metric becomes a blocker
Post-launch review: good for learning what worked, what to kill, and what to scale. Weakness: often skipped unless the org has discipline
Interview line: In healthcare AI, I would never optimize adoption without safety, quality, and workflow guardrails

front: AI / healthcare-specific methods: what matters most?
back:
Human-in-the-loop design: good for regulated workflows, clinician trust, and high-stakes decisions. Weakness: slower than full automation when the task is truly low-risk
Model cards / dataset cards: good for transparency, provenance, limitations, and intended use. Highly relevant to RSNA ATLAS. Weakness: documentation alone does not make a model safe or useful
RAG evaluation framework: good for RSNA + Rad AI — retrieval precision, citation accuracy, groundedness, and usefulness. Weakness: does not evaluate the full clinical workflow by itself
Risk management / ISO 14971 thinking: good for identifying hazards, severity, probability, and mitigations. Weakness: too heavyweight for early ideation unless scaled appropriately
Design controls: good for traceability from user needs → requirements → verification → validation. Essential for FDA-regulated products. Weakness: overkill for non-device educational content unless it touches clinical decision-making
Interview line: For AI at point of care, I’d evaluate the model, the retrieval, the user workflow, and the clinical risk separately

front: How to sound senior when discussing PM frameworks.
back:
Do not list every framework you know — pick the right one for the product stage and risk profile
Discovery: use JTBD, customer interviews, contextual inquiry, and riskiest-assumption testing to understand the problem before committing engineering
Prioritization: use RICE / value-vs-effort / MoSCoW as decision aids, not decision substitutes
Delivery: use agile for iteration, but in regulated digital health pair it with design controls, V&V, risk management, and launch-readiness gates
AI products: add eval methods for retrieval quality, groundedness, hallucination risk, human-in-the-loop review, and guardrails
Best senior line: Frameworks help teams reason together, but product judgment decides what to do when the framework and reality disagree

back:

What drew me to RSNA Ventures: there's a clean overlap between what you're building — taking trusted, peer-reviewed content to the point of care — and what I've spent the last decade doing
At Dexcom I learned how to ship FDA-regulated software reaching a million patients
At Sony I learned how to ship AI products with partners
RSNA Ventures is the role where those two skills converge
Practice 3x out loud. Time it. Cut at 90 seconds.

front: Q1: Years of digital PM experience + types of products.
back:
13+ years in digital product/program management — 2013 to today
Three product types I've owned end-to-end:
Regulated digital healthcare — 5+ years at Dexcom on the FDA-cleared Class II SaMD portfolio: CGM mobile apps (iOS/Android), wearable platforms (Apple Watch, Wear OS), cloud services, sensor hardware/firmware, and SDK/REST APIs. About 1M paying patients globally as MAU
External enterprise SaaS — at ServiceNow, the Service Mapping product protecting Fortune 500 ACV revenue (Microsoft, Qualcomm). At UMPG, a global digital recording library used by music supervisors, agencies, and studios
AI/data-driven products — most recent two years: Sony AITRIOS edge-AI sensing for Fortune 500 retail and Industry 4.0; Intuit Data & AI Acceleration, currently delivering an agentic AI planning capability with a 27-document knowledge base across 40+ stakeholders
The thread: external, customer-facing, revenue-generating products in environments where trust and adoption matter

front: Q2: Walk me through a recent product end-to-end (use Dexcom G6 Pro app).
back:
At Dexcom I led the G6 Pro app from concept to commercial launch — a Class II FDA-cleared SaMD product targeting two external audiences: healthcare providers prescribing CGM, and the ~88M-person U.S. pre-diabetic segment
Concept — started with VoC research and clinical input. What gap did Dexcom's standard consumer G6 leave for a clinician-prescribed, temporary-wear use case? Wrote the opportunity case and the PRD
Definition — made scope tradeoffs explicitly: what the G6 Pro app needed at launch versus what could come later. Authored detailed software requirement specifications including IEC 62304 considerations
Build — cross-functional alignment across firmware, hardware, mobile, wearable, cloud, UX, Marketing, Commercial, and Customer Support. Daily PM ownership: PRDs, user stories, MoSCoW prioritization, value-vs-effort tradeoffs. Partnered with UX on final designs and led technical-feasibility discussions across engineering disciplines
UAT and pre-launch — iterative dev/test cycles through Release Candidate. Partnered on design controls and risk management. Aligned launch readiness across every function for an FDA-regulated release
Launch — general availability for the B2B/B2B2C audience, prescribed by HCPs to patients with insurance reimbursement
Outcome — this was Dexcom's pathway into the prediabetic and HCP-prescribed segments, a strategic expansion beyond the consumer G6 core. The broader G6/G7 portfolio I helped lifecycle-manage was a key factor in Dexcom's growth to a $45B market valuation
What I learned — in regulated software the launch-readiness scorecard across all functions, not just engineering, is the actual schedule. The minute one function slips, regulatory risk compounds. That discipline transfers directly to AI products that need clinical trust

front: Q3: What stages of the product lifecycle have you personally led?
back:
I've personally owned every stage:
Discovery — VoC research at Dexcom; customer interviews at Sony; data discovery at Intuit
Definition — PRDs, software requirement specs (incl. IEC 62304), scope tradeoffs at every employer
Build — daily delivery management, agile ceremonies, MoSCoW and value-vs-effort prioritization

I'm strongest in the messy middle — definition through pilot — and in cross-functional launch readiness for regulated products
I've spent comparatively less time in deep field sales motion and M&A integration

front: Q4: Concept-to-operational-support — Dexcom G6 Pro full system.
back:
If they ask Q4 after Q2, open with: "Same product, different scope — Q2 was the app component; Q4 lets me walk you through the full system from concept to operational support." That sentence shows you understand the difference between shipping a feature and owning a platform.
For breadth and depth at every stage, my strongest concept-to-operational-support story is the Dexcom G6 Pro — the first and only single-use, single-session professional CGM system. I owned this from initial concept through FDA 510(k) clearance, commercial launch, and post-market operational support
Concept — started with the question: what gap exists between Dexcom's consumer CGM and the clinical workflow for HCPs who want to assess glycemic variability without prescribing a permanent CGM? Authored the opportunity case and the PRD targeting two external audiences: healthcare providers running in-office diabetes screening, and the ~88M-person U.S. pre-diabetic segment
Worked with Clinical and Regulatory to validate use cases for both blinded mode (10-day retrospective HCP review) and unblinded mode (patient sees real-time data)
Definition — software requirement specifications including IEC 62304 considerations. Architecture decisions around the practice-owned reader, the patient-facing G6 app, and the Dexcom Clarity reporting integration. Explicit MVP scope tradeoffs — what shipped at launch versus what came later
Build — coordinated cross-functional delivery across nine functions: Regulatory Affairs, Quality, Engineering across firmware/hardware/mobile/wearable/cloud, Clinical, UX, Product Marketing, Commercial, and Customer Support. Daily PM ownership: PRDs, user stories, MoSCoW prioritization, value-vs-effort tradeoffs. Partnered on design controls and risk management for every iteration
UAT and pre-launch — iterative dev/test cycles through Release Candidate. Aligned launch readiness across every function for an FDA-regulated release. Coordinated 510(k) submission, achieving FDA clearance October 2019
Launch — general availability for the B2B/B2B2C audience: practice-owned, HCP-administered, with both blinded and unblinded modes. Dexcom's first single-use professional CGM — pioneered a new category
Operational support — post-market surveillance, customer support workflows, ongoing software/firmware updates, HCP training resources. Integrated VoC feedback into iterative improvements
Outcome — first and only single-use professional CGM in the market. Dexcom's pathway into the prediabetic and HCP-prescribed segments. The broader G6/G7 portfolio I helped lifecycle-manage was a key factor in Dexcom's growth to a $45B market valuation
My role at each stage shifted — early I was the framer and gap-spotter; mid-cycle the prioritizer and connector across nine functions; late-cycle the launch-readiness owner and regulatory coordinator; post-launch the operational-feedback steward
The constant was owning the customer outcome end-to-end across the entire product lifecycle

back:
Yes — extensively. Five-plus years at Dexcom as Technical Product Manager for Mobile, Wearable & Cloud — directly responsible for their FDA-cleared Class II Software as a Medical Device portfolio:
CGM mobile apps (iOS, Android), wearable platforms (Apple Watch, Wear OS), cloud services, sensor hardware/firmware coordination, and the SDK/REST API ecosystem
About 1M paying patients globally as monthly active users

front: Q6: AI-supported, data-driven products + problem solved for external user.
back:
Yes — most of my recent work plus a long thread back through Dexcom. Three examples:
Intuit (current) — agentic AI planning capability. Architected and shipped a 27-document knowledge base across 8 PM methodologies, 6-mode planning skill, 5-tier trigger detection, 9 production templates. Solves the problem of moving from ambiguous business goal to a structured AI-assisted plan. Contributed to $55M in incremental paid-media impact across the broader Data & AI portfolio
Sony AITRIOS — edge-AI sensing platform. Drove the full AI lifecycle: data collection, feature engineering, model integration, fine-tuning, RAG pipelines, embeddings, prompt strategy, API delivery. Partnered with Microsoft on Azure OpenAI and Avanade on delivery for Chipotle, Loblaw, 7-Eleven, and other Fortune 500 retailers and Industry 4.0 manufacturers. Customer problem: get from raw camera feeds to enterprise-decision-grade insights inside compressed delivery windows. Improved AI delivery throughput ~25% through CRISP-DM-structured execution
Dexcom — full-stack data-platform vision. Developed and presented a vision leveraging Dexcom's proprietary CGM dataset and AI/ML image recognition for annotation/labeling to address unmet consumer needs. Concept advanced into the product pipeline
The pattern across all three: I gravitate toward AI products where the user's job is high-stakes and the AI's role is to surface evidence faster, not replace judgment
That's exactly the pattern for the Rad AI partnership — RAG-powered content surfacing, not autonomous diagnosis

Both, depending on the project. That mix is the realistic state of any modern AI product
At Dexcom — primarily in-house. Engineering across firmware, hardware, mobile, wearable, and cloud all reported into the product organization. I owned PRDs, requirements, sprint priorities, and customer-facing decisions. Cross-platform integrations with Clarity, Qualtrics, and Google Fit were partner-facing technical work I scoped and ran
At Sony AITRIOS — explicitly hybrid. In-house product team with engineering, data, and design under product. Plus deep partnerships: Microsoft for Azure OpenAI infrastructure, Avanade for delivery support, ecosystem partners for vertical integrations. I owned strategy and customer experience end-to-end; partners owned model layer and certain delivery components
At Intuit (current) — internal product/engineering plus data science partners across Consumer Group, 40+ stakeholders

Surface and distribution come through partners like Rad AI. The PM job is keeping the strategy whole across that boundary. That's exactly the work I've been doing

I want to be direct about the shape of my management experience. Most of my roles to date have been technical product/program management with matrixed cross-functional leadership and mentorship rather than large direct-report counts:
Intuit: orchestrating delivery across 40+ stakeholders and 10 vertical leads
Sony AITRIOS: end-to-end execution leadership across in-house engineering, data, and design plus partner teams at Microsoft and Avanade
Dexcom: cross-functional leadership across nine functions for every FDA-regulated release — Regulatory, Quality, five engineering disciplines, Clinical, UX, Marketing, Commercial, Support
ServiceNow: orchestrated 60+ cross-functional teams across four countries
I've mentored teammates consistently — that's been called out in every role's review cycle — and I've managed partner/vendor teams as if they were direct reports
The honest gap: I haven't run a permanent five-or-more direct-report org reporting to me on a 1:1 cadence. The job posting mentions managing two PMs growing — that's a step into formal people management I'd take on with the operating model I've used for matrixed teams: weekly 1:1s with the IC owning the agenda, bi-weekly career conversations, quarterly performance check-ins, situational coaching from tell-through-delegate, and a 0-30/30-60/60-90 onboarding pattern
I'd ask the team: how does Ventures want this role to balance hands-on PM ownership versus team leadership in the first 12 months — especially as the team grows beyond two PMs?

front: Q9: How would your team describe your leadership style?
back:
Three words my team would use:
Customer-anchored — I bring the team back to "what does the user actually need" when we drift toward internal optimization. At Dexcom that meant standing up Voice of Customer feedback loops across Marketing, UX, and Customer Support that simplified flows and improved CSAT ~15%
Direct — I say no fast and explain why. I'd rather have the hard conversation early than a polite slow one. At Sony AITRIOS that meant scoping MVPs down per customer demo even when stakeholders pushed for more, because compressed delivery windows demanded it
Data-led — I default to dashboards, KPIs, and quantified tradeoffs. Every role since UMPG has had me build the reporting layer the team and execs use to track delivery — Smartsheet WBS, defect burndown, partner deliverable tracking, KPI dashboards
Two more they'd say if pushed: calm under pressure (regulated launches teach that), and pushing for technical depth — I want to know how the AI actually works, not just the product framing. That earns trust with engineering

front: Q10: Lean / startup / fast-paced inside non-profit.
back:
Honest framing: I haven't worked inside a non-profit specifically. But I've worked startup-pace inside very large mature organizations, which I think is structurally the same challenge
Sony AITRIOS — startup-style team operating inside a multinational parent. Daily standups, weekly customer demos, MVP-per-demo scoping, ship-then-fix cadence. Inside a parent company on a very different cadence and risk tolerance
Intuit currently — six concurrent data and AI programs run with startup velocity but inside a public-company governance structure
The hybrid skill that matters: knowing when to operate by startup rules — move fast, ship rough, iterate publicly with customers — versus when to operate by big-org rules — stakeholder alignment, formal sign-offs, brand-level risk review. Sometimes literally hour by hour
My bet on RSNA Ventures: the dynamic is similar — Ventures needs to ship like a startup, but every move coordinates with a 110-year mission-driven institution. Pure startup loses the institutional weight; pure big-org loses velocity. The hybrid is where the most interesting work happens, and I've been doing it for the last two years

— Fast feedback loop: 'not useful' clicks immediately retune the matching for that user
MVP riskiest-assumption test is exactly this engagement rate — promote to GA only if we hit >=40% substantive engagement on surfaced passages

front: Walk through a radiologist's day end-to-end.

PACS: stores and displays the images themselves (DICOM)
Together they drive the reading room
RIS is process; PACS is pixels

Routes studies to the right AI models
Returns findings into the workflow
Presents results to the radiologist

Solves the 'hospitals juggling 5–20 AI vendors' problem
