PE-backed consolidator
Key strategic counterparty for any radiology platform play
US Radiology Specialists is the other major consolidator

front: What is DICOM?
back:
Imaging data + metadata standard
Every modality speaks DICOM
Carries pixel data and study metadata
DICOM SR (Structured Report) carries machine-readable structured reports

front: DICOM vs HL7 — what's the difference?
back:
DICOM: imaging-specific — pixels, study metadata, structured reports for radiology
HL7 (v2 + FHIR): broader clinical / admin data exchange — orders, results, ADT, EHR integration
Overlap exists (DICOM has worklist concepts, HL7 has imaging messages)
Mostly complementary, not competing

front: HL7 v2 vs FHIR — when do you use each?
back:
HL7 v2: legacy pipe-delimited messaging
Still ~80% of installed base — orders, results, ADT
FHIR (R4 / R5): modern REST / JSON API standard
Lets apps embed in EHRs with patient context (SMART-on-FHIR)
FHIR is the future; coexistence with v2 is the multi-year reality

front: What is SMART-on-FHIR?
back:
App-launch framework that runs third-party apps inside the EHR
Uses FHIR for patient + clinical data access
Standard way to embed point-of-care apps in Epic, Cerner / Oracle Health, etc.
Underlying auth is OAuth 2.0 with optional OIDC

front: What is RadLex?
back:
RSNA's controlled vocabulary / ontology for radiology terms
The glue between unstructured reports and structured data
Used to tag content across RSNA properties

front: What is RadElement?
back:
RSNA's library of Common Data Elements (CDEs)
Standardized question / answer pairs that turn report content into machine-readable data
Backbone of registries, downstream analytics, AI training, regulatory submission
Has an API for vendor integration

front: What is RadReport?
back:
RSNA's library of 200+ structured report templates
15+ languages
radreport.org
Templates produce structured data feeding analytics, AI training, registry submission

front: What is the ATLAS AI Data Hub — and how does it tie to FDA?
back:
RSNA's catalog of AI model cards + dataset cards
230+ entries across 31 subspecialties
atlas.rsna.org
Built on the ROADMAP ontology
Trust + transparency layer
Directly supports FDA submission requirements for ML-enabled devices

front: Why is radiology the dominant FDA-AI category?
back:
1,451 FDA-authorized AI medical devices cumulative through 2025
1,104 are radiology — 76% of total
75% of 2025 FDA AI clearances were in radiology
Volume + image-data structure makes it the natural beachhead

front: Name the 8 categories of radiology AI products.
back:
1) Image acquisition / reconstruction
2) Triage / notification
3) Detection (CADe)
4) Characterization (CADx)
5) Quantification / measurement
6) Workflow / orchestration
7) Reporting / generative AI
8) Knowledge / decision support — RSNA Ventures + Rad AI lives here

front: Critical nuance: do production tools generate full radiology reports from raw images?
back:
No — no deployed system today does full image-to-report generation
Production 'report generation' tools work on text — the radiologist's dictated findings
They auto-write impressions, fix grammar, pull macros
Image-to-report end-to-end is the vision (Aidoc CARE moves toward it)
Hallucination, regulatory risk, and liability keep it out of production

front: Why is RAG the right design for the RSNA + Rad AI partnership?
back:
Content updates frequently (new journal articles)
Citations are required for clinical trust
Auditability matters in a regulated environment
No model retraining required
Keeps the source of truth in the corpus — exactly what RSNA contributes

front: When would you use RAG vs fine-tuning?
back:
RAG when: content updates, citations matter, auditability needed, regulatory bar high — the Rad AI + RSNA case
Fine-tune when: consistent style / format essential, base model can't do the task with examples, latency / cost rules out long context
Real answer: usually both — fine-tune for style, RAG for facts

front: Define embeddings and how they work in a RAG system.
back:
Vector representations of text (or other content) for semantic search
Pipeline: chunk → embed → store in vector DB → at query time, embed the query and retrieve top-k similar chunks
Top-k chunks fed to the LLM as grounded context
Vector DBs: pgvector, Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, Chroma

front: How do you evaluate a generative AI healthcare product?
back:
Offline: golden test set — factuality, completeness, hallucination rate; LLM-as-judge with human spot-check
Online A/B: workflow metrics (time-to-sign, edits-per-impression); guardrail metrics
Clinical: independent reader study, AI-assisted vs not
Continuous: drift monitoring, periodic re-validation, customer-reported issues feeding eval set

front: Foundation model selection criteria for healthcare AI?
back:
Latency — interactive UX vs batch
Cost — input vs output token economics
BAA availability — non-negotiable for PHI
On-prem deployment options — for strict data residency
Closed: GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini
Open: Llama, Mistral
Healthcare-tuned: MedPaLM, BioGPT, Med-Gemini
Don't pick on accuracy alone — regulatory + privacy often dominate

front: What are model cards / dataset cards — and how does RSNA standardize them?
back:
Structured disclosures of model performance, intended use, training data, known limitations
RSNA's ATLAS AI Data Hub catalogs 230+ across 31 subspecialties
Uses the ROADMAP ontology
Directly supports FDA transparency expectations for ML-enabled devices

front: Name a 6-layer AI product metrics framework.
back:
Model quality: sensitivity, specificity, AUROC, calibration
Retrieval quality: precision@k, MRR, citation accuracy
Product engagement: DAU, session depth, feature adoption
Workflow impact: time-to-sign, edits-per-impression, follow-up compliance
Clinical outcome: diagnostic accuracy delta, missed-finding rate
Business: ARR, NRR, gross margin, partner-attributed revenue

front: Top FDA-cleared AI vendors by clearance count?
back:
GE HealthCare — 120 (most prolific)
Siemens Healthineers — 89
Philips — 50
Canon — 45
United Imaging — 38
Aidoc — 31
DeepHealth — 28
Rad AI is text-AI on a different reg pathway — closest reference partner for this role

front: What is Aidoc CARE and why is it a milestone?
back:
First FDA-cleared comprehensive foundation model AI for triage (Jan 2026)
97% sensitivity / 98% specificity
Milestone toward image-to-report end-to-end
Still triage-focused, not full report generation

front: Walk through the full RAG pipeline end-to-end.
back:
1) Ingest source content
2) Chunk into ~200–1000 token passages with overlap
3) Embed each chunk with an embedding model into a vector
4) Store vectors + metadata in a vector DB
5) At query time, embed the user's query
6) Retrieve top-k similar chunks (often hybrid with keyword search)
7) Optional: rerank with a cross-encoder
8) Inject retrieved chunks into the LLM prompt with citation markers
9) LLM generates an answer grounded in retrieved context
10) Return answer with citations and a groundedness score

front: What is a vector database and how does similarity search work?
back:
Database optimized for high-dimensional vector similarity (cosine, dot product, L2)
Uses approximate-nearest-neighbor (ANN) indexes
HNSW: most common (graph-based)
IVF: inverted file with quantization
ScaNN: alternative
ANN trades small recall hit for orders-of-magnitude faster search
Examples: pgvector, Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, Chroma, Milvus

front: What is HNSW and why is it the default ANN index?
back:
Hierarchical Navigable Small World — multi-layer graph
Each node has links to nearby vectors
Search starts at the top (sparse) layer, walks toward query, descends as it gets closer
Recall ~95–99% at sub-millisecond latency over millions of vectors
Tradeoff: memory-heavy and slow to insert vs IVF
Best-in-class for read-heavy workloads

front: Chunking strategies for RAG — name the main ones and tradeoffs.
back:
Fixed-size: simple, may split mid-sentence
Recursive character split: respects paragraph / sentence boundaries
Semantic chunking: split where embedding similarity drops — coherent but expensive
Hierarchical / parent-child: embed small chunks for retrieval, feed parent context to LLM — best of both
Document-aware: exploit headings, sections, tables
For RSNA: hierarchical on journal articles is the obvious choice

front: What is hybrid search and why use it?
back:
Combines dense vector retrieval (semantic) with sparse keyword retrieval (BM25)
Vectors win on paraphrase / concept match
BM25 wins on rare medical terms, drug names, acronyms, exact-quote queries
Fuse via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) or weighted score
For clinical content with names like 'Aidoc CARE' or 'PI-RADS v2.1', hybrid materially beats pure dense

front: What is reranking and when is it worth the cost?
back:
Cross-encoder model scores each (query, doc) pair after initial top-k retrieval
Far more accurate than the bi-encoder used for retrieval
Adds 50–200ms latency
Use when top-k accuracy matters and context budget is tight
Skip when corpus is small or context is huge
Tools: Cohere Rerank, BGE-reranker

front: Define hallucination and the main types.
back:
Output that is fluent and confident but factually wrong or unsupported
Intrinsic: contradicts the source you provided
Extrinsic: adds facts not in the source
Closed-domain: wrong about the corpus you control
Open-domain: wrong about the world
RAG primarily attacks intrinsic + closed-domain
Open-domain is harder — needs guardrails + refusal training

front: Six concrete techniques to reduce hallucination in a clinical RAG product.
back:
1) Force citations — every claim must point to a retrieved chunk
2) Faithfulness check — second-pass LLM verifies each claim is supported
3) Lower temperature (0–0.3) for factual responses
4) Refusal training — model refuses if no relevant chunk retrieved
5) Constrained generation — JSON schema, controlled vocabulary
6) Human-in-the-loop on low-confidence outputs

front: What is prompt injection — direct vs indirect?
back:
Adversarial input that hijacks LLM behavior
Direct: user types 'ignore previous instructions and do X'
Indirect: malicious content lives in retrieved data — poisoned PDF / webpage that the RAG retriever pulls in and the LLM treats as instructions
Indirect is the bigger risk for RAG systems
Defenses: input / output filtering, instruction hierarchy, retrieved-content sandboxing, signed source content, structured outputs

front: What is the 'lost in the middle' problem?
back:
Empirical finding (Liu et al. 2023): LLMs attend strongest to context start + end, weakest to middle
Stuffing 50 chunks into long context is worse than retrieving + ordering top 5
Implications for RAG: aggressively rerank, keep context small, place most-relevant chunks at edges

front: Token economics — how do you reason about cost?
back:
Pricing is per-token; output typically 3–5x input
Levers:
— Prompt caching (~90% off static prefixes)
— Smaller model for simple steps (model cascading)
— Batch API for offline workloads (~50% off)
— Reduce retrieved chunks
— Truncate long histories
Streaming reduces perceived latency, not cost
For clinical content: input dominates — cache the system prompt + tool definitions

front: LLM latency vocabulary — TTFT vs TPS vs e2e?
back:
TTFT (time to first token): how long before user sees streaming start — dominated by prompt processing
TPS (tokens per second): generation throughput once streaming starts
End-to-end: TTFT + (output_tokens / TPS)
Streaming hides TTFT in the UI, doesn't reduce compute
Targets for an interactive clinical product:
— TTFT <1s
— TPS >40
— e2e <5s

front: RAG vs fine-tuning vs LoRA vs prompt engineering — when to use each?
back:
Prompt engineering: free, instant, weakest
RAG: best for evolving facts + citations + auditability
Full fine-tuning: expensive, opinionated; best for consistent style / format
LoRA / PEFT: lightweight fine-tune (low-rank adapters) — cheap, swappable, tone over facts
Real systems often layer all four

front: How do you build an evaluation pipeline for a RAG product?
back:
Three layers:
Retrieval eval: golden query / relevant-chunk pairs — recall@k, MRR, nDCG
Generation eval: golden Q/A pairs — faithfulness, answer relevance, context precision (RAGAS, TruLens automate this)
E2E eval: LLM-as-judge with human spot-checks; clinician panel for high-stakes cases
Run on every model / prompt / chunk-strategy change
Track drift over time

front: What is LLM-as-judge — and what are its pitfalls?
back:
Use a strong LLM (GPT-4o, Claude) to score outputs of another model on a rubric
Cheap and scalable vs human eval
Pitfalls:
— Position bias (favors first option in pairwise)
— Verbosity bias (favors longer answers)
— Self-preference (judges its own family higher)
— Calibration drift across model versions
Mitigate: shuffle order, anchor with rubric examples, blind to model identity, validate against human labels quarterly

front: What are guardrails — and what does an LLM guardrail stack look like?
back:
Layers around the model that filter inputs and outputs
Input: PII redaction, prompt-injection detection, scope-of-question check
Pre-generation: refusal if no relevant retrieval
Mid-generation: structured output / schema enforcement
Post-generation: faithfulness / citation check, toxicity, profanity, jailbreak detector
Logging + alerting on guardrail trips
Tools: NeMo Guardrails, Guardrails AI, Llama Guard

front: What is observability for an LLM product?
back:
Capture every (prompt, retrieved chunks, response, latency, cost, user, feedback) tuple as a trace
Uses:
— Debug bad responses
— Build eval datasets from real traffic
— Monitor drift in retrieval quality, latency, cost, refusal rate
Tools: LangSmith, Langfuse, Arize Phoenix, Helicone, Datadog LLM Observability
For PHI: traces must be encrypted, BAA-compliant, access-controlled

front: What is a model card and what should it contain for clinical use?
back:
Structured disclosure document for an ML model
Contents: intended use, out-of-scope use, training data sources + dates + demographics, evaluation datasets + metrics, known limitations, ethical considerations, version, contact owner
For FDA-relevant clinical use, also:
— Subgroup performance (sex, age, ethnicity, equipment vendor)
— Failure modes
— Contraindications
— Performance under distribution shift
RSNA's ATLAS standardizes this for radiology

front: What's actually in a DICOM file — pixels and tags?
back:
Pixel data: the image array (CT slices, MR slices, X-ray frame, etc.)
Metadata tags: structured key-value pairs in a hierarchical dictionary
Key tags: PatientID, StudyInstanceUID, SeriesInstanceUID, SOPInstanceUID, Modality, BodyPartExamined, ImagePositionPatient, ImageOrientationPatient
Transfer Syntax: tells you the encoding (uncompressed, JPEG, JPEG 2000, RLE)
A single 'study' = many series; a series = many instances

front: What does it mean to 'preprocess' a medical image for AI?
back:
Windowing: clip pixel values to clinically relevant Hounsfield range (e.g. lung, bone, brain windows for CT)
Normalization: rescale to a fixed range (z-score or 0–1)
Resampling: standardize voxel spacing across scanners
Resize / crop / pad: feed a fixed input shape to the model
Anonymize: strip PHI tags and burn-in pixel text
Augmentation: rotate, flip, intensity jitter for training
Without this, models break across scanners

front: Common CNN architectures in radiology AI?
back:
U-Net: segmentation (organs, lesions, tumors) — encoder-decoder with skip connections
ResNet / EfficientNet: classification (normal vs abnormal, finding categories)
Faster R-CNN / RetinaNet / YOLO: detection (bounding boxes around findings)
3D U-Net / V-Net: volumetric segmentation (CT, MRI volumes)
nnU-Net: self-configuring U-Net — a strong default baseline

front: Vision Transformers (ViT) and self-supervised learning — why care?
back:
ViT splits an image into patches and applies transformer attention
At scale, beats CNNs on natural images
DINOv2 / MAE: self-supervised — learn representations from unlabeled images
Massive deal for medical imaging where labels are scarce and expensive
Foundation models for radiology (RadFM, BiomedCLIP) build on this paradigm

front: Name the leading medical / imaging foundation models.
back:
Aidoc CARE: first FDA-cleared comprehensive foundation model for triage
Med-SAM: Segment Anything Model adapted for medical imaging
RadFM: 16B-parameter radiology foundation model (image + text)
BiomedCLIP: image-text contrastive model trained on biomedical literature
Med-PaLM M (Google): multimodal medical model
Med-Gemini: Google's medical-tuned Gemini variant

front: 2D vs 3D models — when do you use each?
back:
2D: single slice or projection (chest X-ray, mammography, single CT slice)
— Faster, less data needed, smaller models
— Loses cross-slice context
3D: full volume (CT, MRI)
— Captures anatomy in 3D, better for tumors, cardiac, brain
— GPU memory is the constraint
— Often run as 2.5D (a few adjacent slices) as a compromise
For RSNA-content RAG: irrelevant — text-side. For imaging AI partnerships: critical to know.

front: What are multi-modal medical models?
back:
Models that take both image and text as input — and often produce text
Use cases: image captioning (draft impression), visual Q&A on a study, image-grounded clinical search
Examples: RadFM, Med-PaLM M, Med-Gemini, BiomedCLIP
Center-of-gravity for next-wave radiology AI: image-grounded report generation, with citations and grounding

front: What is federated learning — and why does healthcare care?
back:
Train models across many sites without centralizing raw data
Each site trains locally; only model updates (weights / gradients) are shared
A central coordinator aggregates updates into a global model
Critical when hospitals can't share PHI across institutional boundaries
Frameworks: NVIDIA FLARE, OpenFL, Flower
Caveats: communication overhead, attack surface (gradient inversion), heterogeneous data quality

front: Segmentation and detection metrics — Dice, IoU, FROC.
back:
Dice (F1 for masks): 2 × overlap / (predicted + ground truth)
IoU (Jaccard): overlap / union
— Dice is more forgiving on small structures
FROC: free-response ROC for detection — sensitivity vs false positives per scan
— The standard for lesion detection in CAD
Classification still uses AUROC + sensitivity + specificity
For generative captioning: BLEU / ROUGE / RadGraph F1

front: Calibration vs accuracy — why does it matter clinically?
back:
Accuracy: did you get the right answer?
Calibration: when you say '90% confident', are you actually right 90% of the time?
A model with high accuracy but poor calibration is dangerous — confident wrongness leads clinicians to over-trust
Metrics: Brier score, Expected Calibration Error (ECE), reliability diagrams
Fix: temperature scaling, Platt scaling, isotonic regression
Ask vendors for both

front: Interpretability for medical imaging AI — what tools?
back:
Grad-CAM: class-activation heatmaps showing which pixels drove the decision
Attention maps: where transformers attended
Integrated Gradients / SHAP: feature attribution
Counterfactuals: 'what would change the prediction?'
For regulators + clinicians: explainability is risk mitigation, not optional
Limits: heatmaps highlight where, not why — they're necessary, not sufficient

front: Distribution shift in medical imaging — and why subgroup analysis matters.
back:
Models trained on Hospital A often degrade on Hospital B
Sources of shift: scanner manufacturer (GE vs Siemens), protocol differences, demographic mix, disease prevalence
Fix: train on diverse multi-site data; monitor performance per site post-deploy
Subgroup analysis: report performance by sex, age, ethnicity, scanner vendor — FDA increasingly expects this
A single AUROC number can hide catastrophic subgroup failure

front: Why don't deployed systems generate full reports from images yet?
back:
Hallucination risk is high-stakes — a fabricated finding can drive patient harm
Liability: who is responsible when an AI report is wrong?
Regulatory uncertainty for image-to-text generation as SaMD
Clinical trust: radiologists won't sign reports they can't verify
Workflow: dictation + structured templates already work and are fast
Path forward: image-grounded suggestions with citations and human sign-off, not autonomous generation

front: What is FHIR and what's a Resource?
back:
Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources — HL7's modern REST / JSON standard
A Resource is a structured object representing one clinical concept
Examples: Patient, Observation, DiagnosticReport, ImagingStudy, Encounter, Medication
Each has a stable URL, JSON (or XML) representation, standard CRUD
FHIR R4 is the most deployed; R5 adds capability statements + more

front: Name the FHIR resources most relevant to a radiology / RSNA product.
back:
ImagingStudy: DICOM study reference
DiagnosticReport: final report content
Observation: individual findings, BI-RADS scores, measurements
ServiceRequest: the order
Patient, Encounter, Practitioner: clinical context
Media: snapshots
DocumentReference: PDF / CDA pointers
Bundle: wraps multiple resources
CapabilityStatement: server's supported operations

front: How does SMART-on-FHIR launch work — EHR launch vs standalone?
back:
EHR launch (most common in clinical workflow):
— EHR opens your app with a launch token
— App exchanges token for OAuth access token scoped to current patient + user + encounter
Standalone launch:
— User opens your app directly
— App initiates OAuth against EHR's authorization server
— User picks the patient
Either way: access token + context (patient, encounter, user)
Flow is OAuth 2.0 authorization code with PKCE

front: What are SMART scopes and give 3 examples?
back:
Permission strings requested at OAuth time, bounding what the app can read / write
Format: '{actor}/{resource}.{action}'
Examples:
— patient/Observation.read — read observations for the launched patient
— user/Patient.read — read all patients the user can see
— launch/patient — request patient context
— system/*.read — backend service access
Granular SMART scopes (R2.0+) split read into 'rs' (read+search) and add filtering

front: OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow — the steps in plain English.
back:
1) App redirects user to authorization server (client_id, scopes, redirect_uri, state, PKCE code_challenge)
2) User authenticates and consents
3) Auth server redirects back with one-time authorization code
4) App backend exchanges code (+ PKCE verifier + client secret) for access token + refresh token
5) App calls FHIR API with 'Authorization: Bearer <access_token>'
6) When access token expires, use refresh token for a new one
PKCE prevents code interception in mobile / SPA contexts

front: DICOMweb — what are WADO-RS, QIDO-RS, STOW-RS?
back:
RESTful HTTP APIs for DICOM, replacing legacy DIMSE protocols
WADO-RS: retrieve studies / series / instances / frames / metadata over HTTP (Web Access to DICOM Objects)
QIDO-RS: query / search for studies (Query based on ID for DICOM Objects)
STOW-RS: store new DICOM objects (Store Over the Web)
Let modern web apps integrate with PACS without raw DICOM TCP

front: HL7 v2 message anatomy — segments, fields, ADT vs ORM vs ORU?
back:
Pipe-delimited messages composed of segments (3-letter codes) with fields
MSH = message header (always first)
Common message types:
— ADT: admit / discharge / transfer (demographics + encounters)
— ORM: orders
— ORU: observations / results, including radiology reports
— SIU: scheduling
— MDM: document management
80% of installed clinical messaging is still HL7 v2; FHIR migration is the long arc, coexistence is the reality

front: What are CDS Hooks?
back:
FHIR-based pattern: EHR fires a 'hook' event
Hooks: patient-view, order-sign, medication-prescribe, etc.
Third-party CDS service responds with cards (suggestions, info, alerts)
EHR renders cards inline in the workflow
Stateless, JSON, real-time
Natural pattern for surfacing RSNA content when a radiologist opens a study or signs a report

front: What is Bulk FHIR / Flat FHIR?
back:
FHIR's population-level export operation ($export)
Asynchronous request to an EHR returns NDJSON files
Covers all resources for a patient group, organization, or system
Designed for analytics, registries, AI training data, value-based care
Critical for RSNA Ventures if a product needs to ingest a partner's de-identified imaging report corpus

front: FHIR vs EHR proprietary APIs (Epic / Cerner) — what's the practical difference?
back:
FHIR is the standard surface
Epic (App Orchard / Showroom) and Cerner / Oracle Health (code console) wrap their FHIR endpoints with developer programs, app review, per-customer activation
FHIR-conformant ≠ deployable
You still go through each vendor's marketplace + each customer hospital must enable your app
Integration time: months, not weeks. Plan accordingly.

front: What is PIX / PDQ?
back:
Patient Identifier Cross-reference (PIX): returns the equivalent identifier in another domain
Patient Demographics Query (PDQ): returns demographics
IHE profiles for matching a patient across systems with different MRNs
Critical when integrating across hospital systems, HIEs, or stitching imaging from multiple sites

front: Audit logging requirements for healthcare APIs?
back:
HIPAA Security Rule requires logs of access to PHI: who, what, when, from where, success / fail
Wire formats: FHIR AuditEvent or ATNA (IHE)
Requirements:
— Tamper-evident
— Retained 6+ years
— Regularly reviewed for anomalies
For SMART apps: log every token use, every PHI fetch, every consent change
Non-negotiable in BAAs

front: Sync vs async — when do you use each?
back:
Sync (HTTP request / response):
— User is waiting
— Latency budget <1–2s
— Needs immediate confirmation
Async (queue / webhook / polling):
— Work takes >2s
— May fail and retry
— Fan-out to many consumers
— Runs offline
Healthcare examples:
— Sync: autocomplete in a reporting UI
— Async: 30-min batch FHIR export, AI inference on a large study, indexing a new journal article

front: Webhook vs polling — and how do you secure a webhook?
back:
Polling: client repeatedly asks server for updates
— Wasteful but simple, works through firewalls
Webhook: server POSTs to client's URL on event
— Efficient, real-time, requires client to expose an endpoint
Secure webhooks via:
— HMAC signature header (server signs payload with shared secret)
— IP allowlist
— mTLS for healthcare
— Idempotency keys so retries don't double-fire

front: What is idempotency and why do APIs need it?
back:
Idempotent operation: same result whether called once or N times
GET, PUT, DELETE are naturally idempotent; POST is not
Networks fail and retry — any non-trivial API needs idempotency keys (client-generated UUID in a header)
Server recognizes duplicates and returns the same response
Critical anywhere a duplicate would charge twice, file twice, or order an imaging study twice

front: Caching layers in a typical web product?
back:
Browser cache (HTTP headers, service workers)
→ CDN edge cache (CloudFront, Fastly)
→ Reverse-proxy cache (nginx, Varnish)
→ Application cache (Redis, Memcached)
→ Database query cache
→ Database itself
Move work as close to the user as you can while keeping correctness
LLM products add: semantic prompt cache + exact prompt cache

front: Cache invalidation — strategies and the famous joke.
back:
TTL: time-to-live — simplest, accepts staleness
Write-through: write to cache + DB simultaneously
Write-behind: write cache immediately, DB async (risky)
Cache-aside: app reads from cache, falls back to DB and populates
Tag-based purge: invalidate by group
Phil Karlton: 'There are only two hard things in computer science: cache invalidation and naming things.'
Most cache bugs are stale reads after a write

front: CAP theorem in plain English.
back:
In a distributed system, when a network partition happens (P, inevitable), choose between:
— Consistency: everyone sees the same data
— Availability: every request gets an answer
You can't have all three.
CP systems (most SQL, MongoDB strict, etcd): refuse writes during partition
AP systems (Cassandra, DynamoDB default, eventual stores): keep accepting writes, reconcile later
Healthcare critical-path data (orders, meds): typically wants CP
Analytics / search: typically wants AP

front: ACID vs BASE.
back:
ACID (traditional RDBMS): Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability
— Every transaction either fully commits or fully rolls back
— DB is always in a valid state
BASE (NoSQL / distributed): Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency
— Accepts temporary inconsistency for higher availability and scale
Modern healthcare systems mix both:
— Postgres ACID for clinical orders
— DynamoDB BASE for activity feeds and search

front: Microservices vs monolith — the honest tradeoff.
back:
Monolith: simple to build, deploy, debug; one DB; scale by replication
— Best for small teams, early-stage products
Microservices: independent scaling, polyglot, team autonomy
— Pay with operational complexity (service mesh, distributed tracing, eventual consistency, data ownership)
Most successful 'microservices' stories started as a well-modularized monolith
Conway's Law: architecture mirrors org chart — split services along team boundaries

front: Why use a message queue (Kafka, SQS, RabbitMQ)?
back:
Decouple producers from consumers
Buffer bursts so a slow consumer doesn't take production down
Enable retries on transient failures
Fan-out one event to many consumers
Provide an audit trail of events
Common patterns: order-event topic feeds analytics + billing + audit; AI inference job queue; webhook delivery queue with exponential backoff
Tradeoff: introduces eventual consistency and ordering complexity

front: Multi-tenancy patterns for healthcare SaaS — silo, pool, or hybrid?
back:
Silo: separate DB / infra per customer hospital
— Strongest isolation, easiest BAA / data residency, expensive
Pool: one shared DB with tenant_id column on every row
— Cheapest, hardest to isolate
Hybrid: pooled compute, siloed data — separate schemas or per-tenant DBs behind a shared app
For a healthcare product crossing dozens of hospitals with different BAAs and data residency rules, hybrid is the typical landing place

front: Three pillars of observability — and how they differ.
back:
Logs: discrete events with timestamps and structured fields
— Best for forensic 'what happened to this request?'
Metrics: numeric time-series (counters, gauges, histograms)
— Best for 'is the system healthy right now?' and alerting
Traces: causal chain of spans across services for one request
— Best for 'why was this request slow?'
Modern stack: OpenTelemetry SDKs → collector → backend (Datadog, Grafana, Honeycomb)
Add LLM-specific traces capturing prompt / response / cost

front: SLI vs SLO vs SLA vs error budget.
back:
SLI (Service Level Indicator): a measurement — e.g. p99 latency, success rate
SLO (Service Level Objective): a target on the SLI — e.g. 99.9% of requests succeed monthly
SLA (Service Level Agreement): a contract with consequences when missed
Error budget: 1 - SLO
— Burn it early in the month, freeze risky deploys until reset
— Lets eng + product trade reliability for velocity transparently

front: Rate limiting — algorithms in plain English.
back:
Token bucket: bucket fills at constant rate; each request takes a token
— Allows bursts up to bucket size, then throttles
— Most APIs (Stripe, AWS) use this
Leaky bucket: requests drain at a fixed rate; overflow dropped or queued
— Smoother, no bursts
Fixed vs sliding window: count requests per minute; sliding avoids boundary-crossing burst
Always: return 429 with Retry-After

front: How would you architect 'RSNA content embedded in Rad AI Reporting' end-to-end?
back:
Content pipeline:
— Ingest journals + RadReport templates + ATLAS metadata
— Hierarchical chunking, embed, hybrid index (vector + BM25)
— Tag with RadLex / RadElement codes for filtering
Retrieval API:
— Takes clinical query (or active dictation context) + filters (modality, body part)
— Returns top-k passages with citations
Inside Rad AI:
— Rad AI calls API during dictation, receives passages
— Optionally formatted with their LLM into a sidebar
Telemetry:
— Log retrieval + click + dwell + dismiss (no PHI in our logs)
Update path:
— Re-embed corpus when journals add issues
— PCCP-style for any model updates
Multi-tenant:
— Pooled app, segregated audit logs by tenant

front: ETL vs ELT — what's the difference and why does it matter?
back:
ETL: Extract → Transform → Load (transform in a separate engine before loading)
— Older pattern, predates cheap warehouse compute
ELT: Extract → Load → Transform (load raw, transform in the warehouse)
— Modern default with Snowflake / BigQuery / Databricks
— Faster iteration, raw data preserved, transforms versioned in SQL (dbt)
Healthcare twist: PHI may need to be transformed (de-identified) before load — pushing back to ETL for that pipeline

front: Data warehouse vs data lake vs lakehouse?
back:
Data warehouse: structured, schema-on-write, optimized for SQL analytics
— Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Synapse
Data lake: any format, schema-on-read, cheap object storage
— S3, ADLS, GCS — raw / semi-structured / unstructured
Lakehouse: warehouse semantics on a lake substrate
— Databricks (Delta), Iceberg, Hudi
— Single source of truth for both BI and ML
For a content + AI product: lakehouse is the modern default

front: Why columnar formats (Parquet, ORC) for analytics?
back:
Columnar: data stored column-by-column instead of row-by-row
Why:
— Compresses better (repeated values per column)
— Reads only the columns you need
— Vectorized query execution
Formats: Parquet (most common), ORC (Hive-native), Avro (row-based, used for streaming)
Query engines: Presto, Trino, Spark, Athena, BigQuery — all native to columnar

front: Streaming vs batch — when do you use each?
back:
Batch: process N hours of data at once on a schedule
— Higher throughput, simpler, lower cost
— Good fit: nightly journal index refresh, analytics rollups
Streaming: process events as they arrive
— Low latency, harder to operate, exactly-once semantics are tricky
— Good fit: real-time alerts, fraud detection, user activity feeds
Stack: Kafka / Kinesis (transport) + Flink / Spark Streaming (processing)
Many teams start with batch; add streaming where latency demands it

front: What is CDC (Change Data Capture)?
back:
Stream changes (inserts, updates, deletes) from an OLTP database into downstream systems
Reads the DB's write-ahead log instead of querying
Preserves order, exactly-once semantics with checkpoints
Tools: Debezium, Fivetran, AWS DMS
Use cases: keep a warehouse fresh, populate an event stream, feed an audit log, sync to a search index
For RSNA: a way to pipe content updates from an editorial CMS into the vector DB

front: What is dbt and why has it become the default transform layer?
back:
Data Build Tool — version-controlled SQL transforms inside the warehouse
Key ideas:
— Tests on data quality (not_null, unique, accepted_values)
— Lineage graph generated automatically
— Documentation co-located with code
— Modular models (one SELECT per file)
Makes the warehouse a software-engineering artifact, not a Wild West of SQL
Fits ELT-style data stacks

front: Data quality — what dimensions matter?
back:
Accuracy: does it match reality?
Completeness: are required fields present?
Consistency: same value across systems?
Freshness: how recent is the data?
Uniqueness: any unintended duplicates?
Validity: does it conform to schema / types?
Lineage: where did it come from? — auditable in regulated domains
For clinical data: completeness + freshness + lineage usually dominate audits

front: AWS HIPAA-eligible services — what does that mean?
back:
AWS publishes a list of HIPAA-eligible services; only those can store / process PHI
You must sign a BAA with AWS
Commonly used: S3, EC2, RDS, Aurora, DynamoDB, Lambda, SQS, KMS, Bedrock (selected models)
Non-eligible at given times: some preview / non-GA features, certain managed integrations
Always check the latest list before designing a PHI flow
Azure and GCP have analogous BAA-eligible service lists

front: Azure healthcare services — what's relevant?
back:
Azure Health Data Services: managed FHIR API, DICOM service, MedTech (IoT for medical devices)
Azure OpenAI: GPT-family models with BAA, regional deployments, no training on customer data
Azure Cognitive Services: language, vision, document intelligence
Azure Synapse / Fabric: warehouse + lakehouse
Microsoft is investing heavily in healthcare; Nuance acquisition makes Azure the home of PowerScribe + DAX

front: GCP Healthcare API — what's relevant?
back:
Cloud Healthcare API: managed FHIR stores, DICOM stores, HL7 v2 stores
Vertex AI: model training, deployment, RAG pipelines
Med-PaLM and Med-Gemini are Google's healthcare-tuned LLMs (limited access)
BigQuery: warehouse-native analytics on de-identified data
Looker: BI on top
Less penetrated in US hospitals than AWS / Azure but strong in research and academic medicine

front: AWS Well-Architected Framework — the 6 pillars.
back:
1) Operational Excellence — running and monitoring systems
2) Security — protecting data, systems, identities
3) Reliability — recovery, failure handling, scaling
4) Performance Efficiency — using compute resources well
5) Cost Optimization — avoiding unnecessary spend
6) Sustainability — minimizing environmental impact
Useful as a structured checklist for architecture reviews
Azure and GCP have analogous frameworks

front: Serverless vs containers vs VMs — when do you pick each?
back:
VMs (EC2, Azure VM): full control of OS, persistent
— Best for legacy workloads, GPU training, deep system access
Containers (ECS, EKS, AKS, Cloud Run): packaged app + dependencies
— Default for most modern services
Serverless (Lambda, Functions, Cloud Run): no infra to manage, pay per invocation
— Best for event-driven, bursty, low-traffic workloads
— Cold-start latency matters
For a content RAG API: containers (with autoscaling) is the typical sweet spot

front: Network isolation for healthcare — VPC, PrivateLink, VPC peering?
back:
VPC: virtual private cloud — your own isolated network in AWS / Azure / GCP
PrivateLink (AWS) / Private Endpoint (Azure): connect to a managed service over private network, never traversing the public internet
VPC peering: connect two VPCs, traffic stays on the cloud backbone
Customer-managed keys (KMS): you hold the keys; cloud never sees plaintext
PHI flows almost always require: VPC + PrivateLink + KMS + audit logs

front: Why does Infrastructure as Code (IaC) matter for regulated software?
back:
IaC: define infra in version-controlled code (Terraform, Pulumi, CDK, Bicep)
Why regulated environments love it:
— Auditable history of every change
— Reproducible environments — staging mirrors prod
— Drift detection — manual changes flagged
— Code review applies to infra, not just app
— Disaster recovery: rebuild the world from the repo
For SaMD: IaC integrates with design history files and audit trails

front: Encryption at rest vs in transit — and what makes them different?
back:
In transit: data moving over a network — TLS 1.2 / 1.3 (HTTPS, mTLS, FHIR endpoints, S3 calls)
At rest: data on disk
— Disk-level: LUKS, EBS encryption
— App-level: envelope encryption with KMS-managed data keys
Healthcare must do both
What audits care about: key rotation, KMS access logging, separation of duties between data engineers and key custodians

front: OAuth 2.0 vs OIDC — what's the difference?
back:
OAuth 2.0: authorization — what can the app do?
— Issues access tokens scoped to resources
OIDC (OpenID Connect): OAuth + an authentication layer
— Adds an ID token (JWT) describing who the user is
SMART-on-FHIR uses OAuth flows with optional OIDC
Quick mental model:
— OAuth answers 'can this app read this patient's data?'
— OIDC answers 'who is the user logged in?'

front: JWT — what is it and what are the common pitfalls?
back:
JSON Web Token: base64url-encoded {header}.{payload}.{signature}
Header: algorithm (RS256, HS256)
Payload: claims (sub, exp, iss, scopes, custom)
Signature: proves issuer
Pitfalls:
— 'alg: none' attack — always pin algorithm server-side
— Long expiries make revocation painful — use short access tokens + refresh tokens
— Don't put secrets in payload — it's signed, not encrypted
— Validate iss, aud, exp on every request

front: PHI handling — the principles you must demonstrate.
back:
Minimum necessary: only the data your feature needs
Least privilege: RBAC + ABAC
De-identification when possible: Safe Harbor 18 identifiers, or Expert Determination
BAA before any vendor sees PHI
Encryption at rest + in transit
Tamper-evident audit logs
No PHI in logs / metrics / LLM training data unless explicitly contracted
Breach notification SLA per HHS rules
Annual risk analysis — ISO 27001 / HITRUST helps

front: OWASP Top 10 highlights every PM should recognize.
back:
Broken access control (most common)
Cryptographic failures
Injection (SQL, NoSQL, LLM prompt injection)
Insecure design
Security misconfiguration
Vulnerable / outdated components — SBOM matters
Identification & auth failures
Software & data integrity failures
Logging & monitoring failures
Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
You don't need to remediate them — you need to know they exist and ensure your team has a process

front: What's an SBOM and why does FDA care?
back:
Software Bill of Materials: machine-readable inventory of every library / dependency in your build
Fields: name, version, license, hash
Standards: SPDX, CycloneDX
FDA's Sept 2023 cybersecurity guidance for medical devices requires SBOMs in premarket submissions
Lets you respond fast to a Log4j-style vulnerability:
— Query 'do any shipped devices include log4j-core 2.x?'
— Get an answer in seconds

front: CI/CD pipeline anatomy.
back:
Source: git push
→ CI build: compile, lint, type-check, unit tests
→ Security scans: SAST, SCA, secrets, container scan
→ Integration tests
→ Staging deploy
→ Smoke tests
→ Manual approval gate (for healthcare / regulated)
→ Production deploy: canary or blue-green
→ Post-deploy verification
→ Rollback hook on SLO breach
For SaMD: every prod deploy needs a documented design history file entry

front: Blue/green vs canary vs rolling deploys.
back:
Blue/green: full duplicate environment, switch all traffic with one DNS / LB flip
— Instant rollback, but 2x infra (expensive)
Canary: route 1% → 5% → 25% → 100% to new version, watching SLOs at each step
— Best for risk reduction, what most modern teams use
Rolling: replace instances one at a time
— Cheapest, slowest rollback
For a clinical AI feature, canary with strict SLO gating is standard

front: Feature flags — why every PM should know them cold.
back:
Decouple deploy from release
Wrap new code paths in 'if flag.enabled(user)'
Lets you:
— Ship dark, dogfood internally
— Beta with named hospitals
— A/B test
— Percentage rollout
— Kill switch on incident
— Geo-target, license-tier-gate
Tools: LaunchDarkly, Statsig, Optimizely, OpenFeature spec
Tech debt risk: stale flags accumulate — schedule cleanup
For regulated AI: flag turns are audit events

front: Incident response — what good looks like.
back:
Severity levels (SEV1–SEV4) with clear examples
On-call rotation with paging
Incident commander role separate from responders
Real-time comms channel + status page
Customer comms via predefined templates
Time-bounded SLA per SEV
Blameless postmortem within 5 days: timeline, root cause, action items with owners + dates, lessons
Track action-item completion rate as a leading indicator of reliability culture

front: What is SaMD?
back:
Software as a Medical Device — IMDRF framing recognized by FDA
Software intended for medical purposes that performs them without being part of a hardware device
Risk-classified by intended use + healthcare situation severity

front: 510(k) vs De Novo vs PMA — when do you use each?
back:
510(k): substantial equivalence to a predicate device
— Most common, fastest
De Novo: novel low-to-moderate-risk device with no predicate
PMA: highest-risk Class III devices
— Requires clinical data
— Most expensive and longest pathway

front: What is IEC 62304?
back:
Medical device software lifecycle standard
Classifies software safety:
— Class A: no injury possible
— Class B: non-serious injury possible
— Class C: serious injury or death possible
Drives the rigor of design, testing, and risk management activities

front: What is ISO 14971?
back:
Medical device risk management standard
Identifies failure modes
Quantifies severity × probability matrices
Drives design controls and post-market surveillance

front: What is QMSR — and when does it take effect?
back:
Quality Management System Regulation — FDA's new QMS rule
Replaces 21 CFR 820 in February 2026
Harmonized with ISO 13485
World's medical device QMS standards now align

front: What is a PCCP?
back:
Predetermined Change Control Plan — FDA mechanism for AI updates
Pre-specifies which model updates can roll out without resubmitting for clearance
The pragmatic way to ship continuously improving AI in a regulated workflow

front: What is GMLP?
back:
Good Machine Learning Practice
Joint FDA, Health Canada, and MHRA guiding principles for ML-enabled medical devices
Covers data quality, model evaluation, transparency, monitoring, human-AI teaming

front: What's the CDS carve-out — and why does it matter for RSNA Ventures?
back:
Clinical Decision Support has a regulatory carve-out from SaMD if it:
— Doesn't analyze images directly
— Is transparent to the clinician (sources / reasoning visible)
— The clinician can independently review
Most RSNA content-delivery products will likely live in this carve-out
Know exactly when the line crosses into SaMD

front: Analytical vs clinical validation vs clinical utility?
back:
Analytical validation: does the algorithm match ground truth on test data?
Clinical validation: does using it change patient outcomes?
Clinical utility: is the outcome change worth the cost / workflow disruption?
All three are needed; many AI products only show analytical

front: HIPAA basics — PHI, BAA, de-identification?
back:
PHI: Protected Health Information
BAA: Business Associate Agreement — required with any partner that touches PHI
De-identification:
— Safe Harbor: remove the 18 listed identifiers
— Expert Determination: statistical method

front: Design Controls — the V-model in one line.
back:
Design Inputs → Design Outputs → Verification → Validation → Design Transfer
Each step has documented evidence
The spine of FDA design history files
How Dexcom shipped G6 / G7

front: AI-specific reimbursement codes — name two examples.
back:
0623T: AI-assisted CT lung nodule analysis
+0775T: AI stroke detection
Levers for hospital reimbursement of AI:
— NTAP (New Technology Add-on Payment)
— TPT (Transitional Pass-Through Payment)

front: JTBD — write a Jobs-to-be-Done statement template.
back:
Template: When [situation], [persona] wants to [motivation], so they can [outcome]
Example: When a radiologist is dictating a complex case, they want to surface evidence-based references quickly, so they can produce a confident, citable impression without breaking flow

front: What is an Opportunity Solution Tree?
back:
Teresa Torres' framework
Structure: outcome at top → opportunities (customer needs / pain points) → solutions → experiments
Forces comparison of solutions against the same opportunity
Prevents bolting solutions onto the roadmap one at a time

front: PRD anatomy — name the 7 sections.
back:
1) Problem statement / JTBD
2) Success metrics
3) Scope (in / out, MVP cuts)
4) User flows / requirements
5) Risks & dependencies
6) Rollout plan
7) Post-launch eval — week 1, month 1, quarter 1, with kill criteria

front: How do you write a PRD specifically for an AI-enabled product?
back:
Standard PRD plus:
— AI choices made and why (model selection, RAG vs fine-tune, eval approach)
— Risks across clinical / regulatory / technical / partner / commercial with mitigations
— Beta cohort + success criteria for GA + kill criteria
— Post-launch eval cadence (week 1 / month 1 / quarter 1)

front: What is a Riskiest Assumption Test (RAT)?
back:
0→1 pattern
Identify the single assumption that, if false, kills the idea
Design the cheapest experiment that tests it
Fake-door, smoke test, Wizard of Oz, signed LOI — whatever proves or disproves it before you build

front: First 90 days as a manager — how do you structure it?
back:
0–30: Listen
— 1:1s with each direct, peers, key partners
— Understand what works and what doesn't; no big changes
30–60: Diagnose
— 2-3 themes (process, talent, prioritization)
— One visible early win
60–90: Decide
— Set the operating model
— Publish team OKRs
— Establish growth plans for each direct

front: Vendor / partner management — the operating playbook.
back:
Joint roadmap doc with shared milestones
RACI for cross-org decisions
Joint steering committee + working-team cadences
Joint success metrics — engagement / revenue, not just 'we shipped'
Clean commercials: customer ownership, revenue booking, support
Co-marketing tied to milestones — RSNA booth, press release, panel

front: Situational Leadership: tell vs sell vs consult vs delegate?
back:
Tell: new hire or clear failure mode (high direction, low support)
Sell: resistance or skepticism (high direction, high support)
Consult: developing IC (low direction, high support)
Delegate: mature IC (low direction, low support)
Match the style to where the person is, not where you'd prefer to be

front: STAR — 'Tell me about leading through ambiguity.'
back:
S/T: Sony AITRIOS edge-AI platform
— Startup-style env, compressed delivery windows
— Partners: Microsoft Azure OpenAI, Avanade
— Customers: Chipotle, Loblaw, 7-Eleven
A: Spearheaded end-to-end execution
— Daily standups
— User stories + acceptance criteria in Jira
— Per-customer MVP scoping
— WBS in Smartsheet
— Reporting dashboards on AI-pipeline readiness, partner deliverables, customer-eval milestones
— CRISP-DM for the AI lifecycle
R: ~25% throughput improvement
— Multiple working integrated PoCs
— AITRIOS adoption supported across retail / supply chain / Industry 4.0
Bridge to RSNA: 'mirrors the Ventures operating model — startup agility inside a larger org'

front: STAR — 'Tell me about influencing without authority.'
back:
S/T: Dexcom — coordinating Reg, Quality, Engineering (firmware, hardware, mobile, wearable, cloud), Clinical, UX, Marketing, Commercial, Customer Support
Across every FDA-regulated release
A: Drove cross-functional alignment
— PRDs as the single source of truth
— Partnered early on design controls + risk management
— Owned the launch-readiness scorecard across all functions
R: G6 and G7 CGM through FDA 510(k) and post-market
— Flagship offerings tied to Dexcom's growth to ~$45B market valuation
— ~1M MAU globally

front: How would you prioritize the next product after the Rad AI partnership?
back:
Three layers:
Customer outcome — what JTBD next? Knowledge at point of care, peer benchmarking, structured-reporting auto-population, international workflow?
Strategic fit for RSNA — leverages content + standards moat, scales membership relevance?
Commercial fit for Ventures — partner-led vs build vs invest, B2B2C vs direct, revenue model (license, subscription, royalty, sponsorship)
Then: 2-3 candidate bets, riskiest assumption for each, propose a 6-week discovery sprint on the top bet

front: How would you measure success for the Rad AI partnership?
back:
Adoption: % of Rad AI Reporting users viewing RSNA-sourced content; sessions / user / week
Engagement quality: dwell time, citation click-through, useful-content rating
Workflow impact: reduces time-to-sign-off? reduces edits to AI-drafted impressions?
Clinical: does surfaced content change the report (caught a missed differential, added a citation)?
Strategic: RSNA brand reach via Rad AI install base; member acquisition / retention lift; partner-attributed revenue
Partner health: joint NPS, support ticket volume, joint pipeline

front: Curveball — 'A senior radiologist on the Board says your product feels untrustworthy. What do you do?'
back:
Listen first — what specifically erodes trust? (Source attribution? Hallucination? Privacy? Workflow disruption?)
Map their concern to product levers:
— Citations
— Confidence scoring
— Opt-in vs default-on
— Transparent model cards via ATLAS
— Human-in-loop review
— Audit logs
Propose a small time-boxed pilot at the Board liaison's home institution with shared success metrics
Frame: 'Your trust is the product. If we can't earn it from RSNA's leaders, we can't earn it from the field.'

front: Curveball — 'Eng says the AI eval will take 6 weeks. Launch is in 4. What do you do?'
back:
Don't shortcut clinical safety
Three levers to evaluate together:
— Reduce scope: smaller, lower-risk subset launch
— Parallelize eval: more expert reviewers, automated harness
— Move launch: co-author a revised joint plan with partners
Surface to leadership early
Worst answer: 'We hit the date but the eval was incomplete.'

front: Your strongest regulated-healthcare story?
back:
Dexcom: FDA-cleared Class II SaMD portfolio (G6, G7)
— 510(k), design controls, risk management
— Aligned across Reg / Quality / Engineering / Clinical / UX / Marketing / Commercial
~1M paying patients globally
Tied to the company's growth to a ~$45B market valuation

front: Your day-of opening — first 60 seconds.
back:
'Thanks for the time. I'm coming in excited because there's a clean overlap between what RSNA Ventures is building — taking trusted, peer-reviewed content to the point of care — and what I've spent the last decade doing: shipping external, customer-facing healthcare and AI products end-to-end.
At Dexcom that meant FDA-cleared SaMD reaching about a million patients.
At Sony AITRIOS it meant standing up a startup-style AI delivery model with partners like Microsoft and Avanade.
I'd love to dig into where Point of Care goes next.'

front: How do you onboard a new direct report?
back:
0-30 / 30-60 / 60-90 framework
Weekly 1:1s (30 min, IC owns the agenda)
Bi-weekly career conversations or rolling growth plans
Quarterly performance check-ins
Start from their stated growth goals + a clear scope ramp
Match coaching style (Tell / Sell / Consult / Delegate) to where they are

front: How do you build a team roadmap with 2 PMs and 4 product lines?
back:
Reduce surface area: 1-2 bets per quarter per PM, kill the rest until capacity opens
Pair PMs with stakeholders, not products, so context compounds
Use shared rituals (joint reviews, joint discovery) to keep the team operating as a unit, not silos

front: TECH — 'Walk me through how you'd architect content embedding into Rad AI's workflow.'
back:
Three layers:
Content pipeline
— Ingest RSNA journals + RadReport + ATLAS
— Hierarchical chunking, hybrid index (dense + BM25)
— Tagged with RadLex / RadElement codes for filtering
Retrieval API
— Scoped endpoint for Rad AI: takes dictation context + filters, returns top-k passages with citations + confidence
— No PHI flows back to us; only opaque session IDs
Embedded UX
— Rad AI renders results as a sidebar / inline suggestion
Telemetry: log dwell, click, dismiss; weekly content-team review of low-engagement passages
Update path: PCCP for any retrieval-model change; corpus refresh on each journal issue

front: TECH — 'How do you handle PHI flowing through a third-party LLM?'
back:
Default: don't send PHI
— Most clinical RAG products only need de-identified clinical content + a non-PHI query (modality, body part, finding type)
When PHI is unavoidable:
— BAA with the LLM vendor — only OpenAI Enterprise, Azure OpenAI, AWS Bedrock, Anthropic via specific contracts qualify
— PHI redaction at the edge before the call
— Disable training-data retention; request zero-retention API mode
— Encrypted prompt logs with strict access
— Document the data flow in your DPIA / risk file
— Prefer on-prem or VPC-isolated open models for true PHI cases

front: TECH — 'How do you measure success of a RAG-based clinical product?'
back:
Layered:
Retrieval quality: recall@k, MRR, citation accuracy on a clinician-built golden set
Generation quality: faithfulness, answer relevance, hallucination rate, refusal-when-uncertain rate
Workflow impact: time-to-sign-off, edits-per-impression, follow-up compliance
Engagement: sessions / user / week, content-rated-useful, citation click-through
Clinical: reader-study evidence that surfaced content changes the report or catches a missed finding
Business: partner-attributed ARR, NRR, retention
Eval pipeline runs on every model / prompt change; clinician-panel review monthly

front: TECH — 'Build vs buy a vector database. How do you decide?'
back:
Buy (managed: Pinecone, Weaviate Cloud) when:
— Team is small
— Time-to-market matters
— Scale is uncertain
Build / self-host (pgvector, Qdrant on K8s) when:
— Data residency / BAA constraints
— Deeply integrated with existing Postgres
— Cost at scale
— Custom hybrid retrieval needs
Pragmatic default for an early-stage RSNA Ventures product:
— pgvector inside an existing Postgres until ~10M vectors or multi-region needs — then revisit
Decision framework: cost + control + capability + speed

front: TECH — 'Tell me about a time you pushed back on a technical decision.'
back:
Frame:
1) The proposed approach + why eng favored it
2) Your concern, grounded in product / customer / risk evidence (not opinion)
3) Reframed the conversation — shifting from 'engineering preference' to 'measurable outcome' (latency, cost, hallucination rate, reg risk)
4) The compromise / pivot you landed on
5) The outcome — measurable
Narrative arc: respect the engineer's expertise, hold the ground when product / customer evidence calls for it, document the decision so it's not relitigated

front: TECH — 'How do you have productive technical disagreements with engineers?'
back:
Get curious before defensive — ask 'what's the constraint that led you here?'
Make it about the user, not the architecture — 'help me understand what the radiologist will experience'
Bring evidence: data, customer quotes, competitive context, regulatory text
Trade scope, not principles — 'what's the smallest version that proves this?'
Disagree and commit when you've made the case and lost — and be honest in the postmortem
Don't relitigate quietly; raise concerns explicitly
Engineers respect PMs who own technical context and don't hide

front: Total compensation anatomy — what to ask about (and in what order)?
back:
Base salary
Annual bonus / target bonus % + payout history
Long-term incentive (rare in non-profit / RSNA Ventures — but ask)
Signing bonus
Relocation
Retirement: 401(k) / 403(b) match + vesting; pension if any
Health + dental + vision + disability + life
Paid time off, sick, parental leave, sabbatical
Professional development budget
WFH stipend, equipment, conference travel
Ask for the full package in writing before counter-negotiating

front: Don't give the first number — why does anchoring matter?
back:
Anchoring: the first number stated dominates the rest of the negotiation
Whoever names a number first sets the reference point
If you give a low number, you've capped yourself
If you give a high number that they can't meet, you risk withdrawal
Response to 'what are your salary expectations?':
— 'I'd love to learn more about the scope before naming a number — based on my Dexcom and Sony scope, I'd expect to be in the upper range of the band you have for this role.'
Make them go first when you can

front: Public band $145–165K — how do you use it without quoting it?
back:
You know the band; don't reveal that you do
Let them surface a number first; if asked, anchor on scope:
— 'Given the Senior PM + Team Lead scope, multiple product lines, and the people management piece, I'm targeting the upper end of comparable PM-Lead bands — roughly $160-175K base + standard benefits.'
If they offer mid-band ($155K), counter to top of band ($165K) plus signing bonus
Never quote the public band back at them — looks transactional

front: Counter-range strategy for this role.
back:
Anchor: $160–175K base + comprehensive benefits
Defensible because:
— Dexcom FDA-cleared SaMD scope — directly transferable
— Sony AITRIOS startup-style AI delivery — exactly the operating model Ventures is building
— ServiceNow + UMPG SaaS / B2B revenue scope
— People management + partner / vendor management both required for the role
Flex levers if base hits ceiling:
— Signing bonus
— Relocation (even if remote)
— Retention bonus at year 1
— Extra PTO
— Earlier review cycle

front: Signing bonus and relocation — when to push for them?
back:
Signing bonus: covers the gap when base / bonus won't move
— Typical range: $10–25K for this band; sometimes higher
— Often paid in two tranches with clawback if you leave inside 12 months
Relocation: even for remote roles, some employers offer one-time stipend for moving to be near HQ or to attend kickoffs
Push both when:
— Base is at the ceiling of their band
— You're leaving equity / bonus at current employer
— Start date requires you to wind down current obligations
These are easier yeses than base because they're one-time, not annual

front: Soft levers — what's actually negotiable beyond base?
back:
Vacation days / PTO — often easier than base
Work setup: home office stipend, equipment refresh
Start date — buy yourself rest between jobs
Review cycle — 'can my first review be at 6 months instead of 12?'
Professional development budget
Conference attendance (RSNA Annual Meeting attendance is a no-brainer ask)
Flexible hours / 4-day week / partial remote if hybrid
Direct-report scope — 'will I have a 3rd PM by month 6?'
Most are zero-budget for the employer and high-value for you

front: What is BATNA and why does it change how you negotiate?
back:
BATNA: Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement
— What you'll do if this offer falls through
— Could be: stay at current role, take competing offer, take time off, consult
If BATNA is strong: you can hold firm, walk away if needed
If BATNA is weak: don't bluff — you'll lose
Make your BATNA stronger before you negotiate:
— Run the process at 2-3 places at once
— Have a clear walkaway number
— Know the cost of staying where you are

front: When the offer comes — the 24-hour rule.
back:
Don't accept verbally on the spot
Response: 'I'm really excited. Can you send me the full written offer? I'll review and get back to you within 24-48 hours with any questions.'
Why:
— Buys time to think clearly
— Lets you read the fine print (clawbacks, non-compete, IP assignment)
— Lets other processes finalize if you're running multiple
— Signals professionalism, not desperation
A real employer will not pull an offer because you took 48 hours
If they pressure-close, that's a yellow flag about the culture

front: Multiple offers — how to use them without lying?
back:
Truthful framing only: never invent an offer
If you have one: 'I'm in late stages with another company. I'd genuinely prefer this role, and competitive comp would close it.'
If you don't have one: don't pretend you do
Never name the other company unless asked and you're comfortable
Never name the other comp number unless they ask
Leverage isn't the offer letter — it's their perception that you have options
Lying gets caught and burns the bridge

front: How do you ask for more without sounding ungrateful?
back:
Lead with enthusiasm: 'I'm really excited about this role and the team.'
Anchor on scope, not desire: 'Given the people-management piece and the cross-org partner scope, I was anchoring on closer to $X.'
Make it collaborative, not adversarial: 'Is there flexibility on base? If not, would a signing bonus or earlier review get us closer?'
Ask specifically — vague asks get vague answers
Always frame the ask in terms of fit and value, not need
Close with: 'If we can land here, I'm ready to sign.'

front: Accepting language — the 'I'm in' email.
back:
After verbal agreement, send confirmation in writing within 24 hours
Key elements:
— Restate the agreed terms (base, bonus target, signing, start date)
— Express enthusiasm for the role and mission
— Confirm next steps (offer letter, background check, reference checks)
— Provide a clean signature
Example: 'Hi [name], thank you again for the offer. I'm thrilled to confirm I'll join RSNA Ventures as Assistant Director, Point of Care, starting [date], at $X base + $Y signing. I'll watch for the formal offer letter — happy to move on background and references whenever you're ready.'

front: Common negotiation mistakes — what to avoid.
back:
Accepting verbally before seeing the full written package
Naming a number first when you don't have to
Making it personal — comparing yourself to others or talking about cost of living
Negotiating tiny soft items endlessly — 1-2 levers max, not 8
Pressure-selling: 'I have an exploding offer Friday' — burns goodwill
Accepting without confirming title, scope, and reporting chain in writing
Burning the bridge at your current employer — give graceful notice, no public victory laps

front: Question to ask — about the role.
back: 'Can you walk me through how the Point of Care product line fits into the broader RSNA Ventures portfolio over the next 12-24 months — and what success looks like for this role at the 6-month and 12-month mark?'

front: Question to ask — about strategy.
back: 'The Rad AI partnership is the first marquee deal. What was the framework that led to picking Rad AI over other partners, and how should that framework apply to the next bet? How does Ventures think about build vs partner vs invest?'

front: Question to ask — about measurement.
back:
'How are you measuring success of the Rad AI partnership today, and what would make you decide to expand or pull back?
Where do you see the line between RSNA's editorial / peer-review standards and the speed needed to ship a software product?'

front: Question to ask — about the org.
back:
'What's the operating cadence between Ventures and the parent RSNA — how do decisions get made when they need to involve Board liaisons like Dr. Flanders?
RSNA Ventures is brand new — what's been the biggest cultural learning so far in standing up a startup-style group inside a 110-year non-profit?'

front: Question to ask — about international.
back:
'How does RSNA's international footprint (160+ countries) shape product priorities — do you build with US workflows first and adapt, or design for global from day one?
And what's the relationship between Ventures' commercial work and the standards groups (RadElement, ATLAS, RadLex) — are they upstream / downstream or genuinely paired?'

front: Strong closing question.
back:
'Based on what we've discussed, do you have any reservations about my fit that I can address now?'
Surfaces objections while you're still in the room
Gives you one last shot to clarify
